schema.name: Taypro Solar Cleaning OPEX Pricing
schema.description: Indicative OPEX pricing for robotic solar panel cleaning in India: per-panel-per-cycle rates, monthly bands by plant size, and pay-per-panel-cleaned billing model.

hero.title: Solar panel cleaning cost per MW: OPEX pricing structure
hero.subtitle: How much does solar panel cleaning cost in India? Compare manual crew economics with OPEX: pay per panel cleaned, ₹0.45–0.70 per module per cycle, and indicative monthly bands for 25–300 MW plants.

prose.heading: How OPEX pricing works
prose.p1: Under OPEX, you pay monthly for panels actually cleaned, rates from ₹0.70 per panel per cycle (under 10 MW) down to ₹0.45 on 100 MW+ fleets. Contract terms, SOPs, and programme design are on the OPEX service product page.
prose.p2: A minimum monthly programme fee applies on smaller sites. Tracker plants carry a multiplier versus fixed tilt because of row geometry and cycle complexity. All settlements reconcile against NECTYR telemetry and agreed SOPs.
prose.p3: Use the indicative table below for budgetary planning, then run the ROI calculator with your specific MW, soiling rate, and tariff for a directional payback estimate.

costComparison.heading: Solar panel cleaning cost: manual crews vs robotic OPEX
costComparison.intro: Manual cleaning costs rise linearly with fleet scale, labour, water tankers, fuel, and inconsistent coverage. Robotic OPEX converts cleaning to a predictable per-panel variable cost with verified NECTYR logs.

row0.manual: Crew scaling per MW; high turnover in remote sites
row0.robotic: Fixed operator team per fleet; remote NECTYR oversight

row1.robotic: Waterless dry cleaning, zero tanker dependency

row2.manual: Lump-sum contracts; hard to audit panel-level coverage
row2.robotic: Pay per panel cleaned; NECTYR block-level reconciliation

row3.manual: 1–2 wet washes/month, labour and water limit throughput

row4.manual: Delayed response after dust events; coverage gaps on long rows
row4.robotic: Scheduled cadence; block-level NECTYR proof of coverage

row5.manual: Rising labour, fuel, water; contract renegotiation risk
row5.robotic: Predictable per-panel opex or defined CAPEX+AMC alternative

opexTable.heading: OPEX monthly cost by plant size (fixed tilt, 6 cycles/month)
opexTable.intro: Directional estimates for budgetary planning. Actual pricing depends on soiling study, installation type, cycle count, and site access. Request a site-specific quote for binding commercial terms.
opexTable.footnote: Rates span ₹{minRate}–₹{maxRate} per panel per cycle depending on plant size and {cycles} cycles/month (range {minCycles}–{maxCycles}). Table assumes {moduleWp} Wp modules, fixed tilt. Tracker sites use higher multipliers.

calculator.heading: Calculate cleaning ROI for your plant
calculator.subheading: Enter your MW capacity, soiling rate, tariff, and procurement model (CAPEX or OPEX) for a directional payback estimate.

howTo.name: How to estimate solar cleaning OPEX cost
howTo.description: Steps to calculate indicative monthly robotic cleaning cost for an Indian utility solar plant.
howTo.step0Text: Divide plant capacity (kW) by module wattage (e.g. 540 Wp) to get total panel count.
howTo.step1Text: After a soiling study, choose 3–10 dry cleaning cycles per month based on dust load and PR targets.
howTo.step2Text: Multiply modules × cycles × rate (₹0.45–0.70 per panel per cycle depending on plant size).
howTo.step3Text: Apply tracker or seasonal-tilt multipliers. Compare against manual crew cost and energy gain in the ROI calculator.

faq.heading: Solar cleaning cost FAQ
faq.subheading: Common pricing questions from O&M and finance teams.
faq.q0: How much does solar panel cleaning cost per MW in India?
faq.a0: Indicative OPEX ranges from roughly ₹1.5–4 lakh per MW per month depending on plant size, cycle count (3–10/month), and installation type. Smaller plants may hit a minimum monthly programme fee. Use our table and calculator for directional estimates.
faq.q1: What is the per-panel cleaning rate on OPEX?
faq.a1: Rates range from ₹0.45 to ₹0.70 per panel per cleaning cycle. Larger utility fleets (100 MW+) receive lower per-panel rates. Tracker installations carry a multiplier versus fixed tilt.
faq.q2: Is OPEX cheaper than buying cleaning robots (CAPEX)?
faq.a2: OPEX avoids upfront robot purchase and depreciation, ideal for fast mobilisation and balance-sheet efficiency. CAPEX may have lower lifetime cost on very large fleets with long hold periods. Run both scenarios in the ROI calculator.
faq.q3: Does pricing include operators and spares?
faq.a3: Yes. OPEX is fully managed: robots, operators, spare parts for cleaning fleet, NECTYR reporting, and cleaning SOPs. You pay for panels cleaned, not separate line items.
faq.a4: Billing aligns to NECTYR telemetry, panels cleaned per block, per cycle, with agreed reconciliation rules and monthly settlement reports.
faq.q5: What is the minimum plant size for OPEX pricing?
faq.a5: OPEX is designed for utility plants from about 10 MW upward. A minimum monthly programme fee may apply below certain capacity thresholds.
faq.q6: Can I get a binding quote?
faq.a6: Yes. Share plant capacity, location, installation type, and current cleaning method. Taypro engineers will conduct a soiling study and provide site-specific commercial terms.
faq.q7: How does robotic cleaning cost compare to manual brush cleaning?
faq.a7: Manual costs include labour, water, fuel, and supervision that scale linearly with MW. Robotic OPEX provides predictable per-panel economics, higher cycle frequency, and audit-ready coverage logs, often recovering the cost through PR improvement within months in high-soiling regions.
faq.q8: What drives the difference between ₹0.45 and ₹0.70 per panel?
faq.a8: Plant size (module count), cleaning frequency, and installation type. Larger fleets above 100 MW with higher cycle counts approach the lower band. Small plants and complex tracker geometry approach the upper band.
faq.q9: Is GST included in indicative pricing?
faq.a9: Commercial quotes specify tax treatment. Use contact form for binding proposals with current GST applicability.
faq.q10: How does OPEX pricing compare to manual cleaning contracts per MW?
faq.a10: Manual contracts often appear cheaper on paper but lack cycle frequency, water cost, coverage audit, and PR recovery speed. Run both scenarios in the ROI calculator with your tariff and soiling assumptions.
faq.q11: Can I switch from OPEX to CAPEX later?
faq.a11: Many IPPs start OPEX for fast mobilisation then transition to owned fleets at contract renewal. Taypro supports both models on the same plant with NECTYR continuity.

stats.heading: Solar panel cleaning cost benchmarks in India

card0.label: OPEX rate per panel per cycle
card0.body: Volume-discounted on large utility fleets. Smaller plants under 10 MW start at the upper band.

card1.label: Indicative monthly OPEX per MW
card1.body: Depends on cycle count (3–10/month), installation type, and module count. Use the table below for directional planning.

card2.label: Operators, spares, NECTYR, SOPs
card2.body: No separate line items for labour or robot depreciation on OPEX: you pay for panels cleaned.

card3.label: Monthly programme floor on smaller sites
card3.body: Applies when calculated volume falls below minimum monthly fee thresholds.

steps.heading: How to get accurate solar cleaning cost for your plant
steps.step0Body: MW capacity, module wattage (e.g. 540 Wp), fixed tilt vs tracker, state/soiling context, and current annual cleaning spend if known.
steps.step1Body: Divide kW by module Wp. Choose 3–10 cycles/month based on soiling study or regional benchmarks (higher in Rajasthan/Gujarat).
steps.step2Body: Multiply modules × cycles × rate (₹0.45–0.70). Apply tracker multiplier (1.8×) or seasonal tilt (1.5×) if applicable. Compare table above for sanity check.
steps.step3Body: Enter soiling loss %, tariff, and procurement model. Calculator estimates energy gain vs cleaning cost for CAPEX and OPEX scenarios.
steps.step4Body: Taypro engineers validate assumptions with a soiling study and issue commercial terms reconciled to NECTYR measurement rules.

eligibility.title: When pay-per-panel OPEX pricing makes sense
eligibility.for0: You want fast mobilisation without robot CAPEX on the balance sheet
eligibility.for1: You prefer opex aligned to verified panels cleaned, not lump-sum crew contracts
eligibility.for2: Your plant is 10 MW+ utility scale with 3+ cycles/month economics
eligibility.for3: Finance team needs predictable monthly cleaning line item for models
eligibility.not0: Very long hold period (15+ years) with capacity to own fleet depreciation
eligibility.not1: Smaller C&I sites where minimum monthly fee dominates unit economics
eligibility.not2: You already own robots and only need AMC + NECTYR

proseSecondary.heading: Why solar panel cleaning cost varies by state in India
proseSecondary.p1: Soiling rate drives cycle count, the primary cost multiplier. A 100 MW plant in Rajasthan may need 8–10 dry cycles monthly during pre-monsoon dust season; a similar plant in eastern India may run 4–6. More cycles × more panels = higher monthly OPEX, but also higher PR recovery.
proseSecondary.p2: Tracker installations add 1.8× rate multiplier versus fixed tilt due to row geometry, stow angles, and CRADYL transfer complexity. Seasonal tilt sits at 1.5×.
proseSecondary.p3: Manual cleaning cost benchmarks rise linearly with MW: crew count, water tankers, supervision, and night-shift premiums. Robotic OPEX converts this to a measurable per-panel variable with audit trail.
